beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: So policy learning turns CATE scores into budget-constrained decisions, and regret analysis measures how much oracle welfare I lose to estimation noise, with correlation 0.7 costing 12 percent and 0.9 costing 3 percent. I want to know where to invest. Have Claude (1) simulate random, top-k, and threshold policies on 10,000 users at a 30 percent budget and print the welfare table, (2) add regret against the oracle at CATE correlations 0.7 and 0.9, and (3) tell me whether a better CATE model or a better policy algorithm buys more welfare.

props.command: So policy learning turns CATE scores into budget-constrained decisions, and regret analysis measures how much oracle welfare I lose to estimation noise, with correlation 0.7 costing 12 percent and 0.9 costing 3 percent. I want to know where to invest. Have Claude (1) simulate random, top-k, and threshold policies on 10,000 users at a 30 percent budget and print the welfare table, (2) add regret against the oracle at CATE correlations 0.7 and 0.9, and (3) tell me whether a better CATE model or a better policy algorithm buys more welfare.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: You have CATE estimates for 10,000 users and a budget to treat 30%. Random? Top-k? Optimal threshold? The gap in welfare is not small.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'policy learning — who gets the treatment?'; budget constraint vs welfare maximization

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Simulate three assignment policies — random, top-k by predicted CATE, and threshold — on 10,000 users with a 30 percent budget. Print a welfare comparison table.

props.command: claude "Write a Python policy learning comparison. Simulate 10000 users, true CATE ~ N(0.05, 0.03). A model predicts CATE with correlation=0.7. Budget: treat top 30%. Compare: (1) random 30%, (2) top-k by predicted CATE, (3) threshold policy. Compute actual incremental outcome (true CATE for treated). Print welfare comparison table."

beats.narration_text: Read it. Policy learning converts CATE estimates into decision rules. The key insight: a noisy CATE estimator can still produce near-optimal policies if the correlation with truth is high enough.

props.code:
# policy_learning.py — Claude Code output

# Policy 1: random 30%
random_treated = np.random.choice(n, size=int(0.3*n), replace=False)

# Policy 2: top-k by predicted CATE
topk_treated = np.argsort(cate_pred)[::-1][:int(0.3*n)]

# Policy 3: threshold (welfare-maximizing budget constraint)
# find threshold t* s.t. sum(cate_pred > t*) = budget
threshold = np.percentile(cate_pred, 70)  # top 30%
threshold_treated = np.where(cate_pred >= threshold)[0]

# Welfare = sum(true_cate[treated]) / n

beats.narration_text: The bar chart is stark. Same 30 percent budget. Random delivers 50 incremental outcomes per 1000. Top-k delivers 85. Threshold delivers 88. The optimal policy is 76 percent better than random — with zero additional spend.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: three-bar chart welfare gain per 1000 users

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to add regret analysis — how much welfare is lost relative to the oracle policy that knows the true CATE? That gap measures the cost of CATE estimation noise.

props.command:
claude "update policy_learning.py:
  -> add oracle policy (treat top 30% by TRUE cate)
  -> compute regret = oracle welfare - policy welfare
  -> show regret as function of cate correlation (0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0)"

beats.narration_text: At correlation 0.7, top-k regret is 12 percent of oracle welfare. At correlation 0.9, regret drops to 3 percent. The implication: improving CATE model accuracy from 0.7 to 0.9 correlation is worth more than switching from top-k to threshold policy.
beats.visual_intent: slate: regret vs CATE correlation curve; oracle vs top-k welfare at each correlation level

beats.narration_text: Policy learning is the last mile of causal ML. CATE estimation gives you scores. Policy learning turns scores into binary decisions under a budget. The regret analysis tells you whether to invest in better CATE models or better policy algorithms.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: CATE -> scores -> policy -> welfare; regret as optimization target

beats.narration_text: Your move: take your current campaign targeting logic and formalize it. Compute the CATE-predicted welfare under your current rule. Then run top-k and threshold. If top-k beats your current rule by more than 20 percent, you have a policy problem, not a model problem.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: formalize targeting, run policy comparison, measure regret

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Policy learning is the last mile of causal ML. CATE estimation gives you scores. Policy learning turns scores into binary decisions under a budget. The regret analysis tells you whether to invest in better CATE models or better policy algorithms. Your move: take your current campaign targeting logic and formalize it. Compute the CATE-predicted welfare under your current rule. Then run top-k and threshold. If top-k beats your current rule by more than 20 percent, you have a policy problem, not a model problem.

artifactLines: At correlation 0.7, top-k regret is 12 percent of oracle welfare.

Your move: take your current campaign targeting logic and formalize it.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Policy Learning: Budget-Constrained Welfare Maximization] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?

props.title: Policy Learning: Budget-Constrained Welfare Maximization
props.subline: if top-k beats your current rule by more than 20 percent, you have a policy prob
